coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-initiated luminol chemiluminescence in the absence of added oxidant
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Ying
Li, Baoxin
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports for the first time the chemiluminescence (CL) of luminol in the absence of added oxidant with carbon dots (CDs) as the catalyst. By means of CL spectra, UV–vis spectra, electron spin resonance spectra, Fourier transform infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectra, the possible CL reaction mechanism is proposed. The CL luminophor was still 3-aminophthalate, the CL enhancement of luminol system was supposed to originate from the intrinsic catalytic activity of CDs, which can efficiently catalyze the decomposition of dissolved oxygen to generate superoxide radical anion in luminol solution. Then, the resulting superoxide radical anion reacted with luminol to cause CL emission. Furthermore, the effects of size and surface states on the catalytic activity of CDs were investigated. The results suggested that the catalytic activity of CDs are mainly affected by the surface states, and the size of CDs display little influence on the catalytic activity of CDs. Therefore, the catalytic activity of CDs can be tuned by modulating functional groups on the surface of CDs, which sheds new light on the characteristic of CDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical reduction of CDs with NaBH4
Measurement of quantum yield of CDs
Intermediates study by active oxygen radical scavengers
Possible mechanism for CDs initiated luminol CL
Effect of surface states on the catalytic activity of CDs
Effect of size on the catalytic activity of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275096 ), Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2013SZS08-Z01 ), and Program for Innovative Research Team in Shaanxi Province (No. 2014KCT-28 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the catalytic procedure of CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CL kinetic curves of luminol system without catalyst (A, inset is the magnified kinetic curves) and in the presence of the CDs (B). (b) CDs-luminol system with different mixing orders: (A) CDs injected into the mixture of luminol and NaOH; (B) luminol injected into the mixture of CDs and NaOH; (C) NaOH injected into the mixture of CDs and luminol. The solution conditions were 100μL of 5.0×10–4 M luminol, 100μL of 0.2M NaOH, and 100μL of 0.5mgmL−1 CDs. CL measurement for each concentration were carried out in duplicate. High voltage: −400V.

(a) CL spectrum of CDs-luminol system. Experiment condition: 5.0×10–4 M luminol, 0.2M NaOH and 0.5mgmL−1 CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (A), luminol-NaOH (B) and luminol-NaOH-CDs solution (C). Inset: UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs before the CL reaction (1) and after the CL reaction (2). Experimental conditions: 5.0×10–4 M luminol, 0.2M NaOH and 0.5mgmL−1 CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic CL-generating mechanism for the oxidation of luminol with three major steps.
Charge transfer reaction between luminol as donor and CDs as acceptor.
Possible mechanism for CDs initiated luminol CL without added oxidant.

Effect of N2 and dissolved oxygen on the CL intensity of CDs-luminol system. CL intensity before (A) and after (B) being bubbled with N2 1h. CL signals gradually reach the previous level after the air was introduced to the reactant solutions for (C) 1h and (D) 3h. Experimental conditions: 5.0×10–4 M luminol, 0.2M NaOH and 0.5mgmL−1 CDs. CL measurement for each were carried out in duplicate. High voltage: −400V.

The ESR spectrum of DMPO/
O2 − adduct in CDs-luminol system with different concentration of CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs and r-CDs, (b) XPS C1s analysis of CDs and (c) r-CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Catalytic activities of different CDs: (A) CDs; (B) r-CDs; (C) oxidized r-CDs. The solution conditions were 100μL of 5.0×10−4 M luminol, 100μL of 0.2M NaOH, and 100μL of 0.5mgmL−1 CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (High voltage: −700V). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of r-CDs (High voltage: −400V). Excitation wavelengths of b and c from 310 to 410nm in 20nm increments. (d) Catalytic activities for CDs of different fractions: (A) 200–1000Da; (B) 3000–5000Da; (C)>10,000Da. The solution conditions were 100μL of 5.0×10− 4 M luminol, 100μL of 0.2M NaOH, and 100μL of 0.5mgmL− 1 CDs. CL measurement for each concentration were carried out in duplicate. High voltage: −400 V. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Effects of radical scavengers on luminol-CDs system intensity.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-initiated luminol chemiluminescence in the absence of added oxidant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an 710062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 712000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports for the first time the chemiluminescence (CL) of luminol in the absence of added oxidant with carbon dots (CDs) as the catalyst. By means of CL spectra, UV–vis spectra, electron spin resonance spectra, Fourier transform infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectra, the possible CL reaction mechanism is proposed. The CL luminophor was still 3-aminophthalate, the CL enhancement of luminol system was supposed to originate from the intrinsic catalytic activity of CDs, which can efficiently catalyze the decomposition of dissolved oxygen to generate superoxide radical anion in luminol solution. Then, the resulting superoxide radical anion reacted with luminol to cause CL emission. Furthermore, the effects of size and surface states on the catalytic activity of CDs were investigated. The results suggested that the catalytic activity of CDs are mainly affected by the surface states, and the size of CDs display little influence on the catalytic activity of CDs. Therefore, the catalytic activity of CDs can be tuned by modulating functional groups on the surface of CDs, which sheds new light on the characteristic of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is based on the production of electromagnetic radiation observed when a chemical reaction yields an electronically-excited intermediate, which either luminesces (direct CL) or donates its energy to another molecule responsible for the emission (indirect or sensitized CL) [1], which has been extensively investigated and applied in different fields owing to its high sensitivity, wide dynamic range, rapid analysis and simple instrumentation. Luminol is one of the most popular CL reagents because of its excellent CL characteristic. Nowadays, the luminol-based CL method has been developed to be an important and powerful tool in many fields [2–4]. Unfortunately, this method give us a general impression that they usually involved the added oxidants, such as H2O2, KMnO4, KIO4, NaBrO and K3[Fe(CN)6], to trigger the CL reaction. Although the CL emission was obtained, these strong oxidants would destroy the buffer condition and even oxidize all of the analytes, making the systems lose selectivity and biocompatibility [5,6]. Hence, a new pathway is highly desirable to meliorate the luminol CL systems.

Carbonaceous quantum dots, so-called carbon dots (CDs), have recently emerged as a new class of brightly photoluminescent nanomaterials with their photophysical properties resemble in many respects those commonly found in semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). Compared with traditional QDs, CDs have unique properties, such as excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, without incurring the burden of intrinsic toxicity (because of the absence of toxic metal ions), or elemental scarcity and without the need for stringent, intricate, tedious, costly, or inefficient preparation steps [7–9], which make them promising potential substitutes for QDs in biolabeling and bioimaging [10–14]. However, most of the current research has mainly concentrated on the fluorescence properties of CDs, and other properties of CDs are less developed. In recent years, some efforts have been made for the study of CDs in CL. In this case, the pioneer work initiated by Lin group [15–17] introduced CDs as new type of luminescent reagent in CL systems and opened a new field of CDs CL studies. Subsequently CL concerning CDs has attracted some interest in more recently. Cui et al. [18] reported that CDs could effectively enhance the NaIO4–H2O2 CL reaction, and the electron–hole annihilation in or between the CD + and CD − and the direct energy transfer from (O2)2 ∗ to the CDs were suggested to account for the CDs CL behavior. Zhao et al. [19] found for the first time that the CL behavior of CDs in a strong alkaline solution and without of any CL reagent. In these systems, CDs participated as reductant, luminophors, or energy acceptors. Meanwhile, with extremely large surface area, good biocompatibility, and high adsorption capacity, CDs may have an intriguing potential for catalysis [20–23]. To the best of our knowledge, however, no luminol CL in the absence of added oxidant that directly employed CDs as a catalyst has been reported until now. Moreover, investigations on the catalytic properties of CDs are just in infancy and there is rare attention on the factors influencing the catalytic activity of CDs.

In this paper, we report a novel concept of developing CL behavior of luminol in the absence of added oxidant with CDs as the catalyst. A plausible mechanism involving the reaction between CDs and luminol to form the activated transition complex [CDs···Luminol] which could accelerate electron-transfer processes during the luminol-dissolved oxygen CL reaction is proposed (Fig. 1 ). Utilizing this mechanistic understanding, we have been able to extend this strategy easily to other carbon materials with π-rich electronic structures such as carbon nanospheres, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots to initiate luminol CL in the absence of added oxidant. Meanwhile, CDs containing different kinds of surface oxygen group were obtained by NaBH4 reduction, and the dependence of catalytic activity on the surface states of CDs was investigated. The higher catalytic activity is induced by CO and COOH groups on the surface of CDs. Based on that, both catalytic activity and CL emission could be tuned by modulating surface states of CDs.

A 1.0×10−2 M stock solution of luminol was prepared by dissolving luminol (Sigma, America) in 0.10M sodium hydroxide solution without purification. The luminol solution was stored in the dark for one week prior to use to ensure that the reagent has stabilized. Sodium borohydride and mannitol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Graphite (99%, 320 mesh) and quinine sulfate (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Company. All of the chemicals used, except where noted, were of analytical-reagent grade. Ultra-pure water was prepared using a Milli-Q-Plus system (18.2MΩcm−1) and used in all experiments.

The CL detection was conducted on a model IFFL-D Chemiluminescence Analyzer (Xi’an Ruimai Electronic Sci. Tech. Co. Ltd., Xi’an, China). The fluorescence spectra were performed using a FL-4600 Spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using a U-3900H UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were taken by using a JEM-2100 TEM (Japan Electronics Co., Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis of CDs and reduced CDs (r-CDs) were performed by using an AXIS ULTRA X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Kratos Analytical Ltd.). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were carried out with a Bruker Tensor27 (Bruker optics, Germany).

CDs were synthesized according to the literature [24–26], with some modification. In brief, 2.0g activated carbon (200 mesh) was dispersed in 100mL of HNO3 aqueous solution and sonicated for 10min. The mixture was then refluxed for 24h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the activated carbon suspension was neutralized with NaOH and subsequently centrifuged, the brownish-yellow supernatant containing CDs were further dialyzed against ultra-pure water. The concentration of CDs was calculated to be 2mgmL− 1 according to the amount of solid CDs. When graphite was used as the carbon source, it was refluxed in 100mL of HNO3/H2SO4 mixture. CDs were obtained after dialysis.

CDs were prepared via the microwave synthesis route according to the literature [27]. 20% glucose was mixed with 10mM phosphate, and the solution was then put into a domestic microwave oven and heated for 4min. Finally the color-changed solutions were purified and diluted with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical reduction of CDs with NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.8g NaBH4 was added to CDs solutions, the mixture was stirred at room temperature. Then the solutions were dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 1week to remove the excess reductant. The r-CDs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated using following function [28]:
Φ =
Φ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2

Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm) was chose as a standard. Where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η and A is the refractive index and optical density, respectively. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield.

The CL kinetic characteristics of CDs were obtained by batch experiments, which were achieved by a static system consisted of a glass cuvette and the Chemiluminescence Analyzer. In a typical experiment, 100μL of luminol solution was added to 100μL of NaOH solution (0.2M) in a cuvette first, and then 100μL CDs was injected by a microliter syringe from the upper injection port. The CL signal was measured and recorded by using the IFFL-D Chemiluminescence Analyzer. The blank experiments were similarly carried out only that the CDs were replaced with the same volume of aqueous solution.

The oxidative acid treatment converted some of the particle surface-carbons into oxygen-containing moieties, such as hydroxyl, carboxylic and carbonyl moieties, that made the CDs more dispersible and thus the formed aqueous suspension is more stable. TEM images, as shown in Fig. S1a, indicate that the CDs are spherical in morphology and well dispersed with an average diameter of 5nm. As with the most other CDs, the prepared CDs exhibit a UV–vis absorption band at 280nm (Fig. S1b), attributed to the n–π∗ transition of the CO band and a π–π ∗ transition of the conjugated CC band [29]. The prepared CDs are highly dispersed in water presenting yellow color as shown in Fig. S1b (inset). FT-IR spectrum is employed to examine the surface groups of the dried CDs. As shown in Fig. S1c, the peak at about 3462 and 1637cm−1 are associated with the OH stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively. The bands at 1384 are attributed to the absorption peaks of COO−. These data show that the obtained CDs are mainly composed of graphitic carbon, with abundant COOH groups at their surfaces.

In alkaline media, the oxidation of luminol by dissolved oxygen produces very weak CL emission, while CL signal was greatly enhanced by injection of CDs into the luminol solution. Fig. 2 a shows the kinetic curves of the CDs-initiated luminol CL system. The mixing order of the reagent solutions played a critical role in the light emission. In order to explore the effect of CDs on the system, batch CL experiments were used to evaluate the different orders of injection solutions. No CL signal was recorded when CDs were mixed with luminol or NaOH alone. As for the reaction of CDs, luminol, and NaOH, three different mixing orders were compared in Fig. 2b. It showed that the injection of CDs into the luminol-NaOH mixed solution generated the strongest CL emission. The CL intensity reached the maximum value rapidly. The light remained for about 100 s before being utterly quenched. The CL intensity and lifetime were obviously enhanced. Blank experiments were also carried out, including activated carbon powder and HNO3 solutions with the concentrations used as the synthesis conditions. No significant enhancement effects were found for the controlled solutions. Moreover, the CL intensity of luminol system increased with increasing CDs concentration, indicating the strong CL emission was induced by CDs rather than the associated species present in the synthesis process. To validate the universal enhancement properties of CDs for luminol CL system, CDs obtained from different sources (graphite as start material) and with different approaches (microwave treatment of glucose) were added to the CL system, and they all enhanced the CL intensity to different degrees (Fig. S2).

In order to ascertain the emission species, the CL spectrum was obtained using the modified Hitachi F-4600 spectrofluorimeter (taken off the light source) combined with a flow injection system. The result (Fig. 3 a) showed that the maximum emission peak centered around 425nm. It is obvious that luminophor of the luminol-CDs system is still 3-aminophthalate (oxidation product of luminol) [30], rather than the emission band of CDs. The result demonstrated that the addition of CDs did not lead to the generation of a new luminophor for this CL system. The enhanced CL signals were thus ascribed to the possible catalysis from CDs or any related species.

In order to check what happened to CDs during the CL reaction, we measured the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs before and after CL reaction in the same basic medium. There was no obvious change on the fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra showed that CDs had a maximum absorption peak at 280nm, and luminol in NaOH media had two absorption peaks at 294 and 346nm [31]. The absorption spectrum of luminol-CDs mixture was measured under similar experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 3b, the absorption of the luminol-CDs mixed system was equal to the sum of the light absorption of the two individual systems. The absorbance for CDs did not change after the reaction, which indicated that CDs itself did not be changed during the CL reaction. Therefore, we suggest that no complex was formed between the species and CDs and that it may be involved in the reaction of the luminol system, the enhancement of CL signals may have originated from the catalytic effects of CDs itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intermediates study by active oxygen radical scavengers
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the mechanism of the luminol-based CL reaction was believed to involve the oxygen-related radicals [32] (for example,
O2 −, OH
and other radical derivatives). The
O2 − or OH
is as the important intermediates leading to luminescence. To study the intermediates generated in the CL systems, the effect of different active oxygen radical scavengers on the CL intensity of luminol-CDs systems were investigated. The results are shown in Table 1 . Ascorbic acid is a common free radical scavenger. As a classical reducing agent, it is dehydrogenized by reactive oxygen species to form dehydroascorbic acid [16]. The CL intensity was absolutely inhibited when 2.0×10−4 M ascorbic acid was added to CDs-luminol mixture solutions, which further confirmed that the CL reactions proceeded via a radical pathway. Glutathione (GSH) and NBT are effective radical scavenger for
O2 − [15]. The CL intensity reduced to 5% when 5.0×10−3 M GSH was added into the CDs-luminol CL system. NBT can be reduced to its deep-blue diformazan form by
O2 −. When 5.0×10−4 M NBT was mixed with CDs-luminol solution, there was an obvious color change from yellow to blue. Meanwhile, we found that NBT could inhibit the CL intensity. These results suggested that
O2 − was generated in the CL process. Sodium formate and mannitol are scavenger for OH
. When 1.0×10−4 M sodium formate and mannitol were added to CDs-luminol solutions respectively, no significant change were found for the CL intensity. The results suggested that OH
did not involve in the CL reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for CDs initiated luminol CL
ce:section.ce:para: The major CL-generating mechanism for luminol oxidation in aqueous solution has been summarized by Merenyi and co-workers [33] to occur in three basic steps, as showed in Fig. 4 : (1) oxidation of luminol to the luminol radical, (2) oxidation of the luminol radical to hydroxyl hydroperoxide (α-HHP), and (3) decomposition of α-HHP (with or without the emission of CL). Among the process, step 1 was supposed to be the rate-determining step of luminol CL [31]. During luminol oxidation processes, the presence of oxygen-related radicals as oxidants is expected to occur. As the case for luminol system without the presence of any added oxidants, such oxygen-related radicals were supposed to be generated from dissolved oxygen.

In the absence of a catalyst, the oxidation of luminol by dissolved oxygen in alkaline solution is a relatively slow reaction process, and the CL intensity is relatively weak. So, we speculated that CDs may interact with the reactants or the intermediates of the reaction of luminol with dissolved oxygen, to facilitate the formation of
O2 −.

A density functional theory study demonstrated that the functionalization of graphene surfaces with carboxylic groups is significant in the reduction reaction of H2O2 [34]. Huang and co-workers [35] reported that the similar effect may exist for the peripheral functionalized carboxylic/carbonyl moieties in the CDs, which could be used as peroxidase mimetics for the catalysis of peroxidase substrates TMB oxidation in the presence of H2O2. The CDs also exhibited good electrocatalytic activity towards oxygen reduction reaction in basic medium [36]. These researches demonstrated clearly that the CDs could promote electron transfer between electronic acceptor and electronic donator. So, we suggested carboxylic/carbonyl modified CDs might be catalyze the reduction reaction of dissolved oxygen in the alkaline luminol solution.

Furthermore, CDs were shown to be both excellent electron donators and acceptors [37]. Lin group [38] proved that the CDs did have electron-donating ability toward dissolved oxygen and the superoxide anion was generated by electron transfer process from CDs to dissolved oxygen in concentrated sodium hydroxide (1M NaOH). Inspired by the above researches, we suggested carboxylic/carbonyl modified CDs have electron acceptors ability toward luminol, and an activated transition complex [CDs···Luminol] was formed by transfer electron from lone-pair electrons in luminol amino groups to the π-system of CDs, as showed in Fig. 5 , which make an increase in the electron density and mobility in the CDs. This would result in acceleration of electron transfer from the CDs to dissolved oxygen without concentrated sodium hydroxide (1M NaOH), and the generated
O2 − reacted with luminol anion (L−) to facilitate the formation of luminol radicals (L
). As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , dissolved oxygen first decomposes into
O2 − at the surface of activated transition complex [CDs···Luminol], the resultant
O2 − further oxidize L . in basic media to produce the strong CL emission.

In order to further verify the mechanism of the CL reaction, a series of experiments were performed and the results were as the follows:
(1)
Deaeration experiments: when N2 was bubbled to the reactant solutions for 1h before the reaction, significant changes in CL intensity (dropping to just under 18% of average CL intensity) were observed. On the other hand, CL intensity was resumed gradually, when these reactant solutions bubbled with N2 were placed in the air for a period of time. It would be concluded that dissolved oxygen was involved in the CL reaction (see Fig. 7 ), and it is necessary for CL because it is the source of the superoxide radical. However, when both CDs and luminol-NaOH solution were bubbled with O2 for 20min, the CL intensity showed no detectable changes. It demonstrates that CDs facilitated the
O2 − production so that oxygen dissolved in the solvent was sufficient for CL generation.
(2)
Electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) of the system: in order to further character the generation
O2 − in this CL reaction, the ESR of CDs-luminol system were measured (Fig. 8 ). 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) has been commonly used as a spin trap to identify
O2 −. Although DMPO could react with both
O2 − and OH
, the reaction between DMPO and
O2 − was predominated when the pH value was above 7.7 as reported [39]. Therefore, in the case of our experiment, it is practicable to consider DMPO as a specific spintrap for
O2 −. In this system, the specific 12-line EPR signal of the DMPO-
O2 − adduct [38] was observed. Furthermore, the ESR signal of 0.5mgmL−1 was much higher than that of 0.12mgmL−1. When NBT, superoxide anion scavenger, was added into the system, the ESR signal disappeared. These results further confirmed that
O2 − was the key intermediate product in the CDs-luminol system.
(3)
Influence of structure of electron donors: Lucigenin (N,N′-dimethyl-9,9′-diacridinium nitrate) is known to undergo CL reaction in aqueous alkaline solution in the presence of reductants and oxygen [40]. The mechanism of the reaction may be that the reductants reduce dissolved oxygen to
O2 −, then
O2 − reacts with lucigenin to generate CL. When CDs were injected into the lucigenin-NaOH mixed solution, and no significantly enhanced CL emission appeared. The reason for this effect is that lucigenin is a quaternary-N, it is difficult to form a negative charge of the center, and cannot provide lone-pair electrons for CDs. This would not result in acceleration of electron transfer from the CDs to dissolved oxygen. These results further substantiate our claim that
O2 − generation in the CDs-initiated luminol CL system has been developed based on the charge transfer reaction between luminol as donor and CDs as acceptor.
(4)
Influence of structure of electron acceptors: QDs similar to CDs in many respects. When carboxylic modified QDs were injected into the luminol-NaOH mixed solution, and no CL emission was observed. The possible reason for this effect is that QDs, in the absence of π-system, cannot form an activated transition complex with luminol. As an additional evidence, however, we examined other different forms of carbon materials with the π-rich electronic structures such as carbon nanospheres, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots. The experiment results showed that at the same condition these carbon materials all showed catalytic activity, and could initiate luminol CL in the absence of added oxidant. The findings further confirmed the catalytic properties were related to the π-system of CDs.

According to the above results, CL route of luminol using CDs as the catalyst in the absence of added oxidant are proposed as follows:
(1)
L +
OH
-
⟶
L
-
(2)
CDs +
L
-
⟶
[ C ⋯ L ]
(3)
[ C ⋯ L ] +
O
2
⟶
O
2
-
+ CDs + L
(4)
O
2
-
+
L
-
⟶
L
(5)
O
2
-
+
L
⟶
3 -
APA
∗
(6)
3 -
APA
∗
⟶
3 -APA + hv

Luminol molecules transform into L− under alkaline condition (reaction 1). Activated transition complex [C···L−] was formed through charge transfer reaction between luminol as donor and CDs as acceptor (reaction 2), which could catalyze the reduction reaction of dissolved oxygen to generate
O2 − (reaction 3).
O2 − react with L− to yield L
(reaction 4), and which could react with
O2 − to yield electronically excited 3-aminophthalate anions (3-APA∗) (reaction 5). Subsequent formation of the excited 3-APA∗ emits light when it is relaxed to the ground state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of surface states on the catalytic activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the fluorescence properties of CDs are obviously affected by their surface states [24,41], it is reasonable to question whether catalytic properties of CDs are dependent on the surface group? Which surface groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxy and epoxy would influence the catalytic properties of CDs and are suitable for luminol CL system? With these thoughts in mind, we propose the chemical reduction of CDs by NaBH4 in aqueous solutions. NaBH4 is a common and strong reducing agent that selectively reduces the carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups in nanostructures at room temperature, and significantly increased the amount of hydroxyl group on the CDs without reducing other species (CC and COOH) [24,42,43]. In all NaBH4-reduction experiments, un-reacted NaBH4 and its byproducts were rinsed away from the CDs by dialysis. The color of the CDs solution changed from dark yellow to colorless (Fig. S1b). The oxygen-containing surface groups were investigated by FT-IR spectra of typical CDs and r-CDs. The stretching vibrations of OH, CO, and COO− were observed in both CDs and r-CDs. However, the vibrational absorption bands of CO at 1637cm−1 shifted to 1656cm−1, and its intensity decreased after reduction which indicated that the surface carbonyl groups of the CDs were partly reduced (Fig. 9 a).

To further confirm the nature of the functional groups on the surface of both CDs and r-CDs, we carried out XPS characterization. Fig. 9b shows the XPS spectra of C1s. In the deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of CDs, there were four clear peaks assigned to four types of carbon with different chemical valences: non-oxygenated ring C (284.6eV), carbon in CO bonds (286.4eV), carbonyl C (288.3eV), and carboxylate carbon (OCO) (289.3eV) [44]. After reduction, the carbonyl peaks disappeared in the C1s XPS spectra of the r-CDs, indicating that carbonyl groups were removed (Fig. 9c). Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, the oxygen-related functional groups located on the surface of CDs and r-CDs are different.

To reveal these effects, catalytic and fluorescence properties of CDs and r-CDs were measured. The concentrations of both CDs and r-CDs were kept the same to compare their catalytic and fluorescence properties. According to Fig. 10 a, CDs show a strong CL enhance ability for luminol CL reaction, and after being reduced by NaBH4, the CL intensity of the r-CDs initiated luminol is decreased significantly. On the contrary, r-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission. The CDs show excitation-independent behavior within an excitation wavelength range from 310 to 410nm (Fig. 10b). However, the r-CDs show excitation-dependent behavior within the same excitation wavelength range (Fig. 10c). Meanwhile, the quantum yield of the r-CDs increased from about 2% to 11% and the maximum emission wavelength blue-shifts from 503 to 431nm compared with CDs. The reason for this effect is the chemical reduction increases the amount of hydroxyl groups on the r-CDs while decreasing the number of carbonyl group. This leads to our speculation that carbonyl-functionalized CDs have more suitable surface structure for catalytic activity and thus have better enhance ability for luminol CL reaction.

In addition, the r-CDs were successfully oxidized to form CDs by heating under reflux in nitric acid. After oxidation of r-CDs, the solution color became yellow (Fig. S1b). The emission peak at ca. 431nm is characteristic of r-CDs and it gradually shifts to 503nm (typical for the original CDs), and the CL intensity increased sharply during oxidation. Upon subsequent oxidation of the r-CDs, the CL intensity was restored but the recovery was incomplete because of CDs mass loss during reduction. On the other hands, we prepared the hydroxyls-coated CDs by one-step hydrothermal reaction as the reported papers [45,46], and then the hydroxyls-coated CDs were oxidized with HNO3 as the oxidizing agent to obtain the carbonyls-coated CDs. The surface functional groups and element states of both hydroxyls-coated CDs and carbonyls-coated CDs were characterized by XPS (Fig. S3). We compared the performance of hydroxyls-coated CDs and carbonyls-coated CDs in luminol CL reaction. The results (Fig. S4) showed that the hydroxyls-coated CDs had weak CL catalytic ability, and after being oxidized by HNO3, the CL intensity was increased significantly. These results further substantiate our claim that catalytic property of CDs is related to surface states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of size on the catalytic activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of size on the catalytic properties of the CDs, the prepared CDs were separated with different molecular-weight-cut-off (MWCO) membranes, respectively. Three CDs with different size were obtained and were used to catalyze luminol CL. As shown in Fig. 10d, the CDs obtained with 200–1000, 3000–5000, and >10000Da MWCO display little differences in luminol CL system. Thus, the CDs have size-independent catalytic activity. However, the sizes of CDs can influence their fluorescence behaviors. Their emission wavelengths are 419, 439, and 505nm and showed red-shift when their molecular weight was increased (see Fig. S5), indicating that fluorescence of the obtained CDs is particle size-dependent. It should be noted here that the size-independence of catalytic activity was not interfered by oxidation degree of the CDs, since the degree of oxidation was the same for the particles of different sizes. This was confirmed by the experiments: (1) FT-IR spectra of all the three fractions (i.e., CDs with different sizes) showed no observable change; (2) catalytic activities of these CDs were pH-dependent, that is, their catalytic activities showed optimum in pH 2, and decreased when the pH value of the CDs was increased from 2 to 9. However, their fluorescence wavelengths were independent of pH value of solution.

In this work, an interesting phenomenon is observed that luminol in the absence of the added oxidants can produce the CL emission when mixing carbon dots in NaOH media. The possible CL reaction mechanisms are proposed. The π-rich electronic structure of CDs enables the activated transition complex [CDs···Luminol] was formed through charge transfer reaction between luminol as donor and CDs as acceptor. CDs could accelerate electron-transfer processes during the luminol-dissolved oxygen CL reaction. Furthermore, study of CL behaviors of CDs initiated luminol has indicated that the catalytic activities of CDs was related to their surface states alone, while their catalytic activities were independent on their sizes. To improve catalytic activities of CDs, it is required to tune their surface states. We note that it should be possible to extend this strategy easily to other carbon materials with well established surface chemistry. This study may open the way for a new class of luminol CL without added oxidant with wide applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275096), Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2013SZS08-Z01), and Program for Innovative Research Team in Shaanxi Province (No. 2014KCT-28).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.10.089.
